evidence: Zain's current thesis focuses on 'human support systems' in an increasingly automated world. He identified three key themes: Community Building, Unleashing Ideas, and Tooling and Resources.

Vrijen described himself as becoming a '1% or 0.1% power user' of products he embeds with—someone who dives so deep into a platform that he reaches functionality most users never discover. Yet he simultaneously identifies as 'non-technical'.

The moments that actually create trust are often the unfiltered overlaps that slip through—the random TikTok connections, the chance hallway conversations. We optimize for efficiency, but trust is created by inefficiency.

Maybe the real value of workspace optimization isn't productivity at all—what if it's identity signaling? The monitor isn't about doing more—it's about being the kind of person who cares about the details.
Vrijen has moved 12 cities across 33 years. Perhaps frequent relocation doesn't just make you adaptable—it makes you good at noticing adaptation.

Zain noted staying off social media for years because of the toxic arguments, but wanting to share ideas. Authentic content creates trust slowly and transactionally, whereas clickbait extracts attention quickly and parasitically.
Zain had randomly connected with Logan (Vrijen's co-founder) on TikTok after seeing her content about work and skills.
Vrijen recommended FOHE (Future of Higher Education), a selective free group for mid-to-upper-tier edtech executives. He's about to be announced as NYC co-lead.
Careerspan isn't a replacement for Handshake—it's the 'missing layer' that makes their products defensible. Distribution platforms solved access, but access without signal is noise.
Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone.

The entire Careerspan thesis rests on the premise that behavioral signal (how people think) is more valuable than profile data (what they've done). This challenges the fundamental premise of LinkedIn.

Positioning Careerspan as a strategic hedge for Handshake's declining core business ($200M, only 6% growth). Even if the partnership never becomes a major revenue line, it provides data supremacy and option value.

Emphasizing the 3-person team as an asset (low acquisition overhead) turns a potential weakness into strength. In an era of bloated startups with high burn rates, being lean makes you an attractive acquisition target.
The real value isn't in helping someone get one job—it's in tracking their entire career trajectory and predicting future success. This creates a dataset that compounds in value over time.
Reference being in conversations with several companies (ranging from white labeling to acquisition). Emphasize low overhead (3-person team, not expensive to acquire).
Handshake's core recruiting business: ~$200M revenue, but only 6% growth. Company is moving away from recruiting line of business but still uses it to source PhDs.
Logan seeing surprising social media growth (2,000 followers in 36 hours from LinkedIn breakdown). Educational content about careers and recruiting has organic demand.
Careerspan's positioning around 'elite talent identification' represents a strategic pivot. The real money in recruiting isn't in volume—it's in high-value placements for top 1% talent.
V opened by identifying a key growth priority: 'We need to scale the outbound.' N proposed hiring more SDRs. V committed to escalating: 'I'll talk to the board.'
